One Link
How it works Features Security Share One Get One Link
Security

The math keeps the promise.

A promise is only as strong as the thing it rests on. Ours rests on architecture: every guarantee on this page is built into the protocol itself, verifiable in your tab, and impossible to walk back without rebuilding the product. Trust the math, then trust us.

What we collect

Nothing.

No email. No phone. No analytics. No tracking pixels. No fingerprinting. No cookies. No request logs that contain you. This site has zero third-party requests. The Cloudflare Worker does not write a single thing about who visited or what they downloaded.

If a government asked us tomorrow who downloaded One Link, who paired with whom, or what was sent through the network, the honest answer is "we do not know and there is no way to find out." That is by construction.

Defends against

The attacks we kill at the door.

Passive eavesdropping.

Anyone watching the wire sees ciphertext only. The keys are post-quantum hybrid (X25519 + ML-KEM-768). A quantum computer harvesting traffic today still cannot read it later.

Active impersonation.

Pairing uses a 5-word SAS derived from the shared secret. If anyone tried to sit in the middle, the words on the two devices would not match. The math agreed only because nobody was between you.

Server compromise.

There is no server holding your messages or files. A relay that gets compromised sees sealed boxes addressed to recipients it cannot identify. No archive to steal.

Traffic analysis (optional).

Private mode (one tap) routes through three volunteer relays via Sphinx routing. No single hop sees both ends. Each hop's keys are PQ-blinded and field-witnessed.

Lost device.

Threshold recovery splits your identity across friends. Recover with k-of-n. The math means a leaked share is useless without the others.

Coerced unlock.

Duress mode opens a believable empty account with a second passphrase. The system cannot tell which is the real one.

Where the trust is yours

Where the math hands off to you.

Honest about where the protocol ends and your judgment begins.

The physical space around you.

The protocol secures what travels across the wire. The screen in front of you is yours to guard, the same way it is for any conversation in any room.

The device under your hands.

We sign every binary and ship the source so you can verify what you run. The operating system itself sits below us; choosing one you trust is the choice that makes the rest meaningful.

The people you choose to share with.

End-to-end encryption keeps the channel between just the two of you. What happens after they receive your message is the same human trust every relationship rests on.

The widest of network-watchers.

Three-hop private routing hides the route from anyone who sees one piece of the internet. For an observer who sees the whole internet at once, timing padding raises the cost dramatically; perfect resistance is the goal of the next research push.

Proofs and audits

The receipts.

Formal verification

Critical protocol pieces (pairing handshake, threshold recovery, onion routing) have TLA+ specifications with invariants verified.

Constant-time crypto

Macaroon verification + signature paths are constant-time tested with a 1M-iteration soundness gate. Timing variance under 2%.

Fuzz testing

Nightly fuzz across 42 binaries. 5M iterations per release. Adversarial vector library grows on every red-team finding.

Reproducible releases

Every release is built in a confidential-compute environment. Attestation document published alongside the binary. Anyone can rebuild from source and verify byte equality.

Prove it

Mint a hybrid identity, right here in your tab.

One click runs the real ol_pqsig crate (compiled to WASM) inside your browser. It generates a fresh Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65 hybrid keypair, signs a short message, verifies the signature, then flips one bit and shows you both halves refusing to verify. No network call. No server-side trust. The same code path the daemon uses for capability commits.

computing 

      

    

  


  

    

      Threshold recovery
      
Split a secret across five hands. Recover from any three.

      

        One click runs ol_threshold_recovery (Shamir secret sharing over GF(2^8), same crate the daemon uses). Your browser splits a 32-byte secret into 5 shares, then recovers it from any 3, and proves that any 2 cannot. The shares look random because they are random. The secret only exists when 3 of them come together.
      


      

        
        
          
          computing
        
        

      

    

  


  

    

      Forward secrecy
      
Every message gets a fresh key. Compromising one tells you nothing about the next.

      

        One click runs ol_ratchet (per-chunk forward-secret KDF chain, BLAKE3-derived). Your browser generates a fresh chain key, derives six sequential message keys, and shows that they are mutually distinct and that the ratchet refuses to rewind. An attacker who captures the key for chunk 3 cannot reconstruct the key for chunk 4 because the chain step is a one-way hash.
      


      

        
        
          
          computing
        
        

      

    

  


  

    

      Trust on first use
      
Your device is recognized without us knowing who you are.

      

        One click runs ol_hwkey (TOFU software fallback, same trait surface the daemon uses with Secure Enclave / StrongBox / TPM on real hardware). Your browser mints a 32-byte device root on first visit and stores it locally. The button derives a deterministic device fingerprint from that root, then simulates an impersonation attempt with a random key and shows the TOFU store reject it in constant time. No identifier is sent to any server.
      


      

        
        
          
          computing

        One Link
        
A free, private network for messages, files, and devices. Owned by no one. Belongs to everyone.
